coredata.dc:title: Seamless aqueous arc discharge process for producing graphitic carbon nanostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Kim, Sejung
Song, Youngjun
Heller, Michael J.
coredata.dc:description: A seamless aqueous arc discharge process was developed to produce graphitic carbon nanostructures as a novel fabrication process to control the morphological transition at nanoscale. The manipulation of graphitic carbon from zero- (0D) to two-dimensional (2D) nanostructures was achieved by an aqueous arc discharge process proportional to the arc discharge power. The aqueous arc discharge process can also incorporate an oil-in-water (O/W) emulsion in the aqueous dielectric medium, which leads to the plastic deformation of 2D graphene sheets into 3D crumpled graphene spheres at moderate current levels (<5 A). At higher current levels, hollow graphitic carbon nanoparticles were produced by generation of a toluene bubble-template, the nucleation of carbon molecules and subsequent growth into graphitic structures in a single batch.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of graphitic carbon nanostructures by an arc discharge process set-up

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: University of California, San Diego Heller Lab Royalty Fund

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by University of California, San Diego Heller Lab Royalty Fund BNGTTLB. The authors are grateful for Dr. Jennifer Wright for fruitful discussions.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of an aqueous arc discharge process: two graphite electrodes were submerged in DI water (dielectric medium) under 25 V and less than 10 A. (b) Temperature profiles according to current levels, indicating that exfoliation of graphite occurred at less than 5 A, and evaporation of graphite occurred at higher than 5 A. SEM images of (c) 2D graphene sheets, (d) carbon nanotubes (CNTs), and (e) carbon nano-onions (CNOs) (scale bar: 3 μm). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

TEM images of (a) multilayered 2D graphene sheets, (b) CNTs, and (c) CNOs to determine the internal structures (scale bar: 10 nm). (d) Deconvolution XPS spectra of C1s peak for CNOs, CNTs, and graphene from top to bottom. (e) Raman spectra of CNOs, CNTs, and graphene on Si wafers. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Crumpled graphene particles composed of 3–7 graphene layers, produced at a current of 1 A (scale bar: 500 nm). Inset shows the component graphene layers (scale bar: 10 nm). (b) Crumpled graphene spheres composed of ridges and vertices on the surface, produced at a current of 4 A (scale bar: 1 μm). (c) Calculation of mass of crumpled graphene spheres as a function of particle size corresponding to the scaling law with log-log scale. The mass of crumpled graphene spheres is associated with the diameter, shown in the inset. (d) The confinement force corresponding to the mean diameter of crumpled graphene spheres by D = 2.4965. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) TEM images of hollow carbon particles with graphitic carbon walls composed of 3–7 layers (scale bar: 10 nm). Inset shows the aggregates of hollow graphitic carbon particles (scale bar: 500 nm). (b) Size distribution of the particles: red circles and open squares correspond to the inner circles, while open squares correspond to the outer layers of the particles. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Seamless aqueous arc discharge process for producing graphitic carbon nanostructures

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Materials Science Engineering Program, University of California San Diego, La Jolla, CA 92093, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanoengineering, University of California San Diego, La Jolla, CA 92093, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Bioengineering, University of California San Diego, La Jolla, CA 92093, USA

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Nanoengineering, University of California San Diego, La Jolla, CA 92093, USA.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A seamless aqueous arc discharge process was developed to produce graphitic carbon nanostructures as a novel fabrication process to control the morphological transition at nanoscale. The manipulation of graphitic carbon from zero- (0D) to two-dimensional (2D) nanostructures was achieved by an aqueous arc discharge process proportional to the arc discharge power. The aqueous arc discharge process can also incorporate an oil-in-water (O/W) emulsion in the aqueous dielectric medium, which leads to the plastic deformation of 2D graphene sheets into 3D crumpled graphene spheres at moderate current levels (<5 A). At higher current levels, hollow graphitic carbon nanoparticles were produced by generation of a toluene bubble-template, the nucleation of carbon molecules and subsequent growth into graphitic structures in a single batch.

ce:section.ce:para:
Various dimensions of graphitic carbon nanostructures composed of sp
2 -hybridized carbon bonds have been intensively investigated for uses in photo/electrocatalyst, optoelectronics, and energy generation/storage devices [1–6]. Due to the dependency of the Quantum Hall Effect (QHE) on the chemical composition, and dimensional features, the bandgap of two-dimensional (2D) graphene can be manipulated by chemical doping with boron, nitrogen, and oxygen [7–9]. Also, the dimensional morphological features affect the bandgap as a form of zero-dimensional (0D) and one-dimensional (1D) graphitic carbon nanostructures such as fullerene derivatives and carbon nanotubes [10,11]. The synthetic methods for producing graphitic carbon nanoparticles can be largely categorized into top-down and bottom-up processes. The former is based on the exfoliation of graphite into low dimensional carbon nanostructures; exfoliating graphite produces 2D graphene sheets by overcoming the attractive van der Waals (vdW) force, and GQDs can be produced by further dissection of 2D graphene sheets into nanometer-sized objects [12,13]. Conversely, the bottom-up process is based on the formation of sp
2 -carbon bonds by choosing the proper precursors through chemical vapor deposition (CVD) process [14,15]. However, these current synthetic processes have not been developed for producing various dimensional carbon nanostructures in a single batch to meet the dimensional requirements needed for use in various applications. In our previous research, we showed that an aqueous arc discharge process can produce a controlled number of graphene layers as well as degree of oxidation of 2D graphene sheets in a single batch [16,17].

Here, we demonstrate the capabilities of a seamless aqueous arc discharge process for producing dimensionally different architectures of graphitic carbon nanostructures: (1) An arc discharge power dependent process for producing graphitic carbon nanostructures such as carbon nano-onions (0D), carbon nanotubes (1D) and graphene sheets (2D). (2) An oil-in-water (O/W) emulsion assisted arc discharge process to manipulate the morphological features of 0D and 2D graphitic carbons into hollow structures and 3D crumpled graphene spheres.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphitic carbon nanostructures by an arc discharge process set-up
ce:section.ce:para: High purity graphite electrodes were prepared as an anode (6 mm in diameter, 99.999%) and a cathode (12 mm in diameter, 99.999%). Two graphite electrodes were aligned vertically and submerged in DI water (18.2 MΩ), and they are moved up and down so that they contact each other at 2 pps (pulse per second). The initial voltage was fixed at 25 V by a DC power supply (Instek, SPS-3610) and the current was set from 1 to 5 A. For incorporation of O/W emulsions, toluene was injected into an aqueous solution containing 1% w/v polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, Mw ∼50 k) as a dispersed phase at the rate of 50 μl/h. With the same arc discharge procedure as before, graphene sheets and particles were produced.

The point-heat-source model (PHSM) is employed in order to evaluate the temperature by an aqueous arc discharge process. The resulting temperature profiles are given by Ref. [18]:
T =
T 0
+
F c
U I
2 π
κ t
r
erfc
r
2
α t
where T is the resulting temperature, T
0 is the ambient temperature of the graphite (298 K), F
c is the total power fraction of the cathode (0.183), U is the applied voltage, I is the applied current, κ
t is the thermal conductivity of the graphite (2000 W m−1 K−1), r is the size of 2D graphene sheets, t is the pulse duration, α is the thermal diffusivity (α ≡ ρ/C
p ), C
p is the heat capacity of graphite (10.05 J mol−1 K−1), and ρ is the graphite density (2.26 g cm−3). The size of 2D graphene sheets is in the range of 300 nm and 3 μm. In Fig. S1, the resulting temperature is shown as a result of changes in current levels. Current levels from 1 to 4 A can increase the temperature up to 3100 K, which is lower than the sublimation temperature (4000 K). Once the current is applied at 5 A, the temperature achieved is 4000 K, which is assumed to be the threshold of graphite evaporation.

Deformation of 2D graphene sheets can be evaluated by the fractal scaling law by Refs. [19–21],
M ∝
R M
D
where M is the mass of the 2D graphene sheet, R
M is the crumpled particle diameter, and D is the fractal dimension of the crumpled particles. D varies as 2 ≤ D ≤ 3, where D = 2 is a flat sheet, D = 3 is a sphere, and D = 2.5 is for self-avoiding sheets that ideally don't interact with other graphene sheets.

The deformation force is associated with the Young's modulus and the thickness of 2D graphene sheets by,
ρ =
ρ m
(
F
Y h
)
δ D
(
R h
)
3 − D
where ρ is the mass density of a spherical particle, ρ
m is density of GO (1910 kg m−3), Y is the Young's modulus of 2D graphene sheets, R is the particle radius, h is the sheet thickness, D is the measured fractal dimension, and δ is the force scaling exponent for a non-interacting sheet (0.25) [19]. For Y, we used the two-dimensional elastic constant of a single reduced graphene oxide sheet (129 N/m) [22].

The morphological features of carbon nanoparticles were observed using scanning electron microscopy (ESEM, Philips XL30) operated at 10 kV and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tacnai G2, Sphera) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an AXIS Supra (Kratos) photoelectron spectrometer. The Raman spectra were collected with a 532 nm laser using a Reinshaw inVia Raman Microscope.

The experimental setup for our aqueous arc discharge process is represented in Fig. 1 a, which is composed of graphite electrodes submerged into deionized (DI) water. The graphite is used as electrodes (anode and cathode) and as a carbon source in the system, and DI water is used as a dielectric medium. When the electric field is generated by applying a voltage (25 V), the arc discharge is initiated by moving the anode up and down to repeatedly strike the fixed cathode. The plasma zone in the gap between two graphite electrodes produces an electron pathway, which results in heating the surface of graphite electrodes. The resulting graphitic carbon nanoparticles move towards the water surface and assemble laterally, and then can be collected for further characterization.

Graphitic carbon structures can be obtained by either exfoliation of graphite or evaporation and recombination of carbon molecules in an arc discharge process. The conventional arc discharge process, which used high current (>15 A), can achieve the sublimation temperature of 4000 K, resulting in the evaporation of carbon molecules and recombination into specific carbon structures [23]. In our arc discharge process, we assume that the graphitic carbon nanostructures can be obtained without high energy consumption, wherein the arc discharge power is controlled by simply adjusting the current levels below 10 A. The relationship between the current levels and temperature is investigated to optimize the arc discharge power (Fig. S1). In Fig. 1b, the maximum temperature achieved by increasing the current to 4 A is ∼3180 K, which is not enough to evaporate graphite electrodes. However, the temperature is found to be around 4000 K when the current level is raised to 5 A, which indicates the threshold for evaporation temperature of graphite in our system. Therefore, the temperature profile provides insights for the formation of graphitic carbon structures through either evaporation and recombination of carbon molecules or exfoliation of graphite.

The morphological features of the graphitic carbon nanoparticles were examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), as shown in Fig. 1c–e. When the maximum current levels are less than 4 A, 2D graphene sheets are produced with highly wrinkled structures (Fig. 1c). When the current levels are increased to currents greater than 4 A, the nanoparticles with different dimensions were produced as agglomerates composed of tubular shape and sphere-like carbon nanoparticles, as shown in Fig. 1d and e. According to the temperature profiles and the resulting graphitic structures, it is believed that the 2D graphene sheets are produced by exfoliating the graphite electrodes, but the 0D and 1D carbon structures are produced by evaporation of graphite and recombination of carbon molecules. The internal structure was investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). 2D graphene sheets are composed of multiple graphene layers with ∼0.35 nm of an interlayer distance in Fig. 2 a, which is a comparable value to that of pristine graphene [10,11]. Fig. 2b shows that the tubular particles are composed of less than 10 carbon layers, which has proven to be multi-walled carbon nanotubes. Also, the sphere-like nanoparticles are composed of concentric graphitic carbon shells called carbon nano-onions in Fig. 2c.

While an arc discharge in the water is in process, the reactive species (O+, O++) are produced in the plasma zone and increase proportionally according to the arc discharge power [16,17,23,24]. Those reactive species result in the oxidation of graphitic carbon nanoparticles or the formation of gas species such as CO/CO2 [16,17,23,24]. Therefore, we assume that the degree of oxidation of carbon nanoparticles is dependent on the arc discharge power and can be controlled by adjusting the current level in our arc discharge process. X-ray photoemission spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out to estimate the oxygen-including defects as a direct means to verify this oxidation hypothesis.

Deconvolution of high-resolution of C1s XPS spectrum was performed, wherein the graphitic carbon nanoparticles were categorized into graphene, carbon nanotubes (CNTs), and carbon nano-onions (CNOs). In Fig. 2d, deconvolution of C1s spectra for graphene represents that the relative content of the pristine C atoms (284.6 eV) was 88.3%, and that of C atoms in CO (285.9 eV), CO (286.3 eV), and OCO (288.15 eV) are 8.4%, 2.1%, and 1.2%, respectively. For CNTs, deconvolution of high-resolution C1s spectra shows that the deconvolution of C1s spectra for graphene shows that the relative content of pristine C atoms (284.6 eV) was 84.4%, and that of C atoms in CO (285.9 eV), CO, and OCO (288.15 eV) are 10.1%, 3.8%, and 1.7%, respectively. Deconvolution of C1s spectra for CNOs shows that the relative content of the pristine C atoms (284.6 eV) was 64.4%, and that of C atoms in CO (285.9 eV), CO (286.3 eV), and OCO (287.8 eV) 11.8%, 9.7% and 14.1%, respectively. According to the XPS results, it demonstrates that the relative contents of C atoms in non-oxygenated and oxygen-including functional groups is dependent on the current levels during the arc discharge process. Therefore, the aqueous arc discharge process is able to control the degree of oxidation of graphitic carbon nanoparticles through a seamless process by simply altering the arc discharge power.

Owing to the presence of oxygen-including functional groups, we evaluated the quality of the graphitic carbon structures by Raman spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. 2e. The intensities of the D-, G-, and 2D peaks are associated with defects, graphitic carbon bonds, and the number of graphene layers at 1351, 1578, and 2660 cm−1 [25,26]. The relative intensity ratio of ID/IG associated with defects of graphitic structures is decreased with decreasing current: The value of ID/IG is 0.92, 0.32 and 0.13 for CNO, CNTs, and graphene sheets, respectively. The 20 cm−1 up-shifted of the 2D peak of graphene indicates an increase in the number of stacked graphene layers [25]. The degree of defects in graphitic carbon structures is affected by the degree of oxidation, which results from the destruction of graphitic carbon bonding structures by oxygen bonding to carbons.

According to the XPS and Raman results, the degree of oxidation of graphitic carbon nanoparticles is increased with increasing currents during the arc discharge, which indicates that with more reactive species (oxygen ions), there is a higher degree of oxidation. At below 5 A, aside from the oxidation, the rapid heating by an arc discharge can induce water cavitation, which is found to be 17 MPa to the surface of graphite electrodes [27]. The pressure fluctuation is enough to exfoliate the graphite since the minimum exfoliation pressure for graphite is below 2.5 Mpa [28]. Therefore, it is concluded that 2D graphene was produced by thermal expansion and mechanical tension by water cavitation. However, in the case of the CNTs and CNOs, the carbon molecules were produced by high heat along with oxygen ions in the plasma zone and recombined into graphitic structures along with oxygen binding, which results in the oxygen-related defect in CNTs and CNOs.

In our aqueous arc discharge process, the advantages of using DI water are demonstrated by the following: First, it is able to maintain the temperature by cooling the arc discharge system. Second, the rapid heating from the arc discharge leads to the water pressure fluctuation (cavitation), resulting in mechanical tension to the graphite surface for exfoliation followed by flushing the plasma zone. Third, the degrees of oxidation can be adjusted according to the arc discharge power due to the fact that the arc discharge generates a plasma zone including reactive oxygen ions, which results in forming oxygen-including functional groups in the resulting graphitic carbon nanostructures.

The most important aspect of our seamless arc discharge process is to achieve the formation of O/W emulsion in the system for manipulation of dimensions of graphitic carbon structures. A non-ionic surfactant, polyvinylpyrrolidone (M
w , ∼50 k), was used at a concentration of 1% w/v and toluene as a dispersed phase was supplied at the injection rate of 50 μL h−1. Once 2D graphene sheets are produced by an aqueous arc discharge, they are captured inside the toluene droplets or the interfaces between water and toluene due to the hydrophobic properties of graphene [17,29,30]. Then, the O/W emulsion provides the high curvature and deformation force induced by evaporation of toluene by plasma heat followed by capillary compression followed by inducing the deformation of the entrapped graphene sheets [17,29–31].

The morphological features are monitored by varying the arc discharge power at a constant injection rate of toluene (50 μL h−1) in order to investigate the dependency of the degrees of deformation on the concentration of component graphene sheets. Fig. 3 shows the representative crumpled graphene particles with 500 nm and 3 μm in diameters and crumpled and wrinkled surfaces, wherein the graphene was produced at 1 and 4 A, respectively. The crumpled graphene was composed of 3–7 layers (Fig. 3a inset), which indicates that few graphene layers were deformed into crumpled spheres, generating ridges and vertices (Fig. S2 and 3).

The deformation or crumpling of graphene sheets can be evaluated as self-avoidance materials [32,33]. Based on the fractal scaling law, the fractal dimension is obtained by the calculated mass of crumpled spheres as a function of the diameter of the particles (see Supplementary material), which is presented on a log-log scale in Fig. 3c. The mass of crumpled graphene spheres is linearly increased according to the increase in the resulting particles, obtaining D = 2.4965 for crumpled graphene spheres, which is a comparable value for self-avoiding sheets [32]. The confinement force required to deform graphene sheets is evaluated, as shown in Fig. 3d. The deformation force is applied to the crumpled particles at 14 μN, which is inversely increased to the diameter of the particles. As the arc discharge power increases, more graphene layers are formed and overlap at the interface, which results in a higher bending modulus against deformation due to the relationship, F
deformation
αC
graphene
1.57
[33].

While 2D graphene sheets are produced at lower current levels (<5 A) in terms of exfoliation of graphite, the carbon molecules evaporate and recombine into CNOs and CNTs at higher current levels (>5 A), as we can see in Fig. 2. They are expected to assemble or nucleate as graphitic carbon structures from the center to outwards followed by forming concentric graphitic carbon layers. Likewise, in the presence of O/W emulsions, we assume that the evaporated carbon molecules can also be assembled inside of toluene droplets or at the interface between the toluene gas bubble and water. The toluene was supplied into the system at the same injection rate (50 μL h−1) as that of the crumpling process at lower current levels. In Fig. 4 , TEM images show that the sphere-like carbon nanoparticles were produced in the presence of toluene droplets. The internal structures of the particles were hollow with concentric graphene layers. The size of carbon nanoparticles is 12.10 ± 3.08 nm, and 8.36 ± 2.86 nm in the outer and inner shells, respectively. In addition, the wall thickness is 3.74 ± 1.00 nm, indicating that the graphene layers are composed of 5–6 layers with an interlayer distance of ∼0.7 nm, also shown in Fig. S5.

It is assumed that the carbon molecules aggregate and grow at the interface of the toluene gas, which is induced by gas bubble-driven interfacial tension minimization and leads to formation of hollow graphitic carbon spheres [34]. Based on our synthetic process, the formation of hollow spheres could be considered in terms of toluene bubble-assisted assembly of graphitic carbon particles, which is involved in generating a bubble-template (toluene) by arc discharge, the nucleation of carbon molecules, and carbonization with subsequent assembly into graphitic structures. Once the monolayer of graphitic carbon layers is formed, the second and third graphene layers are subsequently deposited on top of the first monolayer. In addition, the PVP in nanospheres formation could play a vital role in the formation of hollow graphitic carbon structures. In the absence of PVP, carbon nanoparticles are produced as a control and those particles are not hollow structures (Fig. S6). Therefore, the presence of PVP layers results in protecting the nanometer-sized toluene bubbles from collapsing and also provides a supportive layer to preventing escaping carbon molecules.

In conclusion, we have developed an aqueous arc discharge process for producing various dimensions of graphitic carbon nanoparticles by adjusting the arc discharge power, obtaining carbon nano-onions, carbon nanotubes, and graphene sheets. The use of DI water as a dielectric medium is capable of controlling the degree of oxidation. More interestingly, employing an O/W emulsion system with an aqueous arc discharge process can lead to the morphological transition of graphene sheets: confinement of the graphene sheets and deformation into crumpled nanospheres. Also, the hollow graphitic carbon structures can be constructed by confining carbon molecules in toluene gas bubbles leading to self-assembly into concentric hollow layered nanostructures. Lastly, efforts are ongoing to use this system for designing graphitic carbon-based nanocomposites with high surface areas for CO2 gas capture and catalytically-active materials.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by University of California, San Diego Heller Lab Royalty Fund BNGTTLB. The authors are grateful for Dr. Jennifer Wright for fruitful discussions.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Supplementary material contains calculation of temperature profiles and deformation force of graphene, and additional TEM, SEM, and AFM images.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2017.04.006.
